title: STANDARD FOR PUMMELOS CXS 214-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR PUMMELOS CXS 214-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document heading: Standard for Pummelos (CXS 214-1999), adopted 1999 and amended 2005 and 2011.

This standard applies to commercial varieties of pummelos (Citrus grandis/C. maxima) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging and excludes pummelos intended for industrial processing.

Sets out pummelo quality provisions: minimum requirements (whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and bruising, appropriate ripeness and transport suitability), maturity/colour and soluble solids criteria, and classification into Extra, Class I and Class II with specific allowed defects and limits for each class.

Minimum requirements require pummelos to be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and bruising, free of abnormal moisture and off-odours/tastes, firm and undamaged by low temperatures, and sufficiently developed to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition; maturity requires minimum total soluble solids of 8% and colouring typical of the variety on at least two-thirds of the surface.

sections.section: 2.1.1 The pummelos must reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness in accordance with criteria proper to the variety and/or commercial type and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Pummelos must reach appropriate development and ripeness per variety and production area and be in condition to withstand transport and handling and to arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.
Minimum total soluble solids must be at least 8%, and colouring must be typical of the variety/commercial type on at least two-thirds of the fruit surface, considering variety and picking time.

Pummelos are classified into three classes: Extra (superior quality, essentially defect-free), Class I (good quality with slight allowable defects subject to a 10% area limit and no pulp damage) and Class II (meets minimum requirements but may have more defects subject to a 15% area limit and no pulp damage).

'Extra' class pummelos must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I pummelos must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety; slight defects in shape, colouring, skin formation or healed mechanical damage are allowed provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation, the total affected area does not exceed 10%, and the pulp is not affected.

Class II includes pummelos that meet the minimum requirements but not higher classes and may show defects in shape, colouring or healed skin defects provided the fruit retain essential quality and presentation; the total affected area should not exceed 15% and the pulp must not be affected.

Size is determined by weight or maximum equatorial diameter according to a specified table of size codes; pummelos may be packed by count (allowing two adjacent size codes), fruits under 400 g or under 100 mm diameter are excluded, and size uniformity is required except for bulk bins and individual non‑rigid consumer packages where the maximum difference may span three consecutive sizes.

Tolerances for quality and size are allowed per package or lot: quality tolerances permit limited percentages of produce not meeting the declared class to fall into lower classes (specified per class) and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight of fruit from the adjacent size above or below.

Quality tolerances by class are specified: Extra class allows 5% (by number or weight) of fruit not meeting the class but meeting Class I; Class I allows 10% not meeting the class but meeting Class II; Class II allows 10% that fail to meet the class or minimum requirements, excluding fruit affected by rotting or unfit for consumption.
For 'Extra' class, up to 5% by number or weight of pummelos not satisfying the class may be permitted provided they meet Class I or, exceptionally, its tolerances.
For Class I, up to 10% by number or weight of pummelos not satisfying the class may be permitted provided they meet Class II or, exceptionally, its tolerances.

Class II pummelos may include up to 10% by number or weight of fruit that do not meet the class or minimum requirements, excluding fruit affected by rotting or other deterioration that makes them unfit for consumption.
For all classes of pummelos, a tolerance of 10% by number or weight is allowed for fruit corresponding to the size immediately above or below the size indicated on the package.
Heading and introductory provisions on presentation of pummelos, with subsequent subsections specifying uniformity, packaging, and container description requirements.
Each package or bulk lot of pummelos must be uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality, size and colour, and the visible contents must be representative of the whole.

Pummelos must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials of quality that avoid damage; non-toxic inks/glues are required for printed materials, and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers for pummelos must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements to ensure suitable handling, shipping and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and smell.
Heading and introductory text on marking and labelling of pummelos, with detailed consumer and non-retail container provisions set out in following subsections.
Introductory provision stating that, in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985), specific labelling provisions for pummelos apply.
If pummelos are not visible from the outside, each package or bulk lot must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety or commercial type.

Non-retail packages of pummelos must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly on the same side and visible from outside, or the particulars must appear in accompanying shipment documents for bulk transport.
Non-retail packages must identify the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
Non-retail packages must state the name of the produce if contents are not visible and may optionally indicate the variety or commercial type.
Non-retail packages must declare the country of origin and may optionally give the district or national/regional/local place name where the pummelos were grown.

Commercial identification on non-retail pummelo packages must declare class, size (size code or min/max weight or diameter), optional net weight, and—for fruit packed in layers—size code(s) and number of fruit; recycled food-grade material is permitted for packaging and a code mark may be used in lieu of explicit name/address if 'packer and/or dispatcher' is indicated alongside it.
An official inspection mark may be applied to pummelo packages (optional).
Pummelos covered by this Standard must comply with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Preparation and handling of pummelos are recommended to follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice, and the produce should comply with any applicable microbiological criteria established under Codex guidance.
